summary.category: How Democratic Institutions Share Power in Decision-Making
points: Major policy decisions require multiple institutions working together. The 1990 reservation policy illustrates the chain: Commission studies issue, Parliament debates, political parties include in manifestos, elections occur, Cabinet decides, government issues order, bureaucracy implements, courts review constitutionality. No single institution alone makes such decisions.

This multi-institutional approach ensures that decision-making is: accountable (multiple bodies must approve), deliberative (multiple perspectives considered), and checked (courts can invalidate unconstitutional decisions). Though slower than single-person rule, this complexity protects against tyranny and ensures democratic legitimacy.

Parliament is supreme in democracy with four key functions: making and changing laws, controlling government (through no-confidence motions forcing resignations), controlling public money (sanctioning all government spending), and serving as highest forum for public debate. Government cannot take major decisions without Parliament's support.

India's bicameral Parliament divides labor: Lok Sabha (directly elected, 543 members, more powerful) controls government and dominates money matters; Rajya Sabha (indirectly elected, 250 members, permanent) represents states. For ordinary laws both must agree; for money bills Lok Sabha is final, reflecting that popularly elected body controlling public money holds most power.

The Prime Minister is the most powerful executive official, appointed by President but requiring Lok Sabha majority support. PM distributes ministerial work, chairs Cabinet, and has final decision-making authority. Cabinet of senior ministers functions as a team with collective responsibility—all must publicly support Cabinet decisions.

Political executive (elected ministers) makes policy; permanent executive (civil servants) implements. Though bureaucrats may have more technical expertise, ministers have final authority because they are electorally accountable to voters. Civil servants continue serving despite electoral changes, ensuring administrative continuity and merit-based governance beyond politics.

Independent judiciary ensures government power is checked and constitutional rights protected. Through judicial review, courts can declare laws or government actions invalid if unconstitutional. Judges are appointed through constitutional procedures and cannot be arbitrarily removed, protecting independence from government pressure.

Public Interest Litigation allows citizens and organizations to approach courts on behalf of public interest, expanding democracy beyond voting. Courts have used this power to protect environment, enforce worker rights, and ensure government fulfills constitutional promises. This demonstrates how institutional independence enables courts to act as check on executive and legislative power.

summary.category: Why Democratic Institutions Matter Despite Inefficiency
points: Democratic institutions involve rules, procedures, and multiple approvals that slow decision-making but protect against tyranny. While a single person could decide faster, institutions ensure: no concentration of unlimited power, multiple perspectives considered, minorities protected from majoritarian oppression, and decisions made transparently and scrutinizable.

The institutional complexity that frustrates citizens—committees, consultations, delays—is essential feature of democracy, not bug. It prevents any single center of power from being absolute and ensures accountability. Citizens must maintain constant vigilance to ensure institutions function properly and independently, as institutional weakness threatens democratic legitimacy.
